DC Crash Spatial Analysis Project Outline

Spatial Equity Analysis of Auto Claims, Crashes, and Roadway Network Conditions in Washington, DC

A tract-level spatial integration framework using ACS demographic boundaries, crash records, roadway network data, and traffic signal infrastructure.

1. Project Objective2. Spatial Backbone3. Data Sources4. Data Integration Workflow5. Avoiding Duplication6. Direct Join Problem7. Correct Integration Strategy8. Dependent Variable9. Feature Engineering10. Next Plan11. Recommended Modeling Direction12. Validation Strategy12. Next Steps13. Summary

1. Project Objective

Analyze crash patterns in Washington, DC using spatial data integration.

Understand relationships between crashes, roadway conditions, traffic signals, and demographics.

Build tract-level dataset for modeling and policy insights.

2. Spatial Backbone (acs_demo)

Dataset: ACS_5-Year_demographic_Characteristics_of_DC_Census_Tracts.shp

Contains: GEOID, tract geometry, demographics, ALAND, AWATER.

Serves as the base layer for all spatial joins.

3. Data Sources

Crashes in DC (point data)

MPDLATITUDE, MPDLONGITUDE, REPORTDATE, crash severity / injury variables

  • MPDLATITUDE + MPDLONGITUDE → point geometry
  • spatial join to acs_demo
  • aggregate by GEOID and time

Traffic Signals (point data)

traffic signal points → spatial join to acs_demo

aggregate signal/control counts by GEOID

Example:

  • signal_count
  • LPI_count
  • HAWK_count
  • APS_count
  • pedestrian_phase_count

Roadway Blocks (line data)

roadway segments → spatial join to acs_demo

aggregate roadway characteristics by GEOID

Example:

  • roadway_segment_count
  • total_road_length
  • mean_speed_limit
  • mean_AADT
  • bike_lane_count
  • bus_lane_count
  • parking_lane_count
  • sidewalk indicators

ACS demographic tract data (polygon)

Base spatial unit for all joins and aggregation

Keyfields:

  • GEOID
  • geometry
  • population variables
  • race/ethnicity variables
  • ALAND
  • AWATER

4. Data Integration Workflow

Convert crash coordinates to geometry using MPDLATITUDE and MPDLONGITUDE.
Spatially join crashes, traffic, and roadway data to acs_demo.
Aggregate each dataset to GEOID level.
Merge all aggregated features into one dataset.

The purpose is to create one consistent modeling unit: GEOID × time or one row = one census tract during one time period

This avoids duplication because each raw dataset has a different geometry type and granularity:

DatasetGeometryRaw Granularity
crashespointone row per crash
traffic signalspointone row per signal
roadway blocklineone row per road segment
acs_demopolygonone row per census tract

By spatially joining each dataset to acs_demo, each feature can be aggregated to the tract level before merging.

5. Avoiding Duplication

Different datasets have different granularity (points, lines, polygons).

Direct joins create many-to-many relationships.

Aggregation to GEOID ensures one-to-one structure.

Pipeline:

Step 1: Spatial join crashes → acs_demo → assign GEOID
Step 2: Aggregate crashes by GEOID/time
Step 3: Spatial join traffic signals → acs_demo → assign GEOID
Step 4: Aggregate traffic features by GEOID
Step 5: Spatial join roadway blocks → acs_demo → assign GEOID
Step 6: Aggregate roadway features by GEOID
Step 7: Merge all aggregated tables by GEOID

Final dataset = one row per tract-time.

GEOID | year | month | crash_count | traffic_features | roadway_features | demographic_features

6. What Happens If Crashes Are Directly Joined to Traffic or Roadway Blocks?

Crash Data

crash_idlocation
C1point

Roadway Block Data

road_idgeometry
R1line
R2line
R3line

If crash C1 intersects or is near 3 road segments:

crash_idroad_id
C1R1
C1R2
C1R3

1 crash → 3 records

Problem: Many-to-Many Duplication

crash_idRoad Segment
C1R1
C1R2
C1R3

One crash joins to multiple road segments, inflating counts.

Same Issue with Traffic Signals

Example:

One crash occurs near two traffic signals:

crash_idsignal_id
C1S1
C1S2

Again:

1 crash → 2 records

7. Correct Integration Strategy

Instead of direct crash-to-road or crash-to-signal joins:

Wrong:

crashes ↔ traffic signals

crashes ↔ roadway blocks

Use:

Correct:

crashes → acs_demo → aggregate by GEOID

traffic signals → acs_demo → aggregate by GEOID

roadway blocks → acs_demo → aggregate by GEOID

then merge aggregated tables

This ensures:

  • one tract = one spatial unit
  • one tract-time row = one modeling observation

8. Dependent Variable

Example:

  • GEOID = 11001000100
  • year = 2022
  • month = 5
  • crash_count = 42

Primary: crash_count (tract-time level)

Alternative: crash_density = crash_count / area_km²

Alternative: binary high-risk classification

9. Feature Engineering

  • Temporal: year, month, weekend, night, cyclical features
  • Crash metrics: crash_count, speeding_count, fatal_crash_count, injury_crash_count night_crash_count, weekend_crash_count, rush_hour_crash_count, severity, ratios
  • Roadway: lane count, speed limit, AADT
  • Traffic: signal counts, control types
  • Demographics: population, total_population, population density, race distribution
  • Spatial normalization using ALAND

10. Next Plan

Step1: Data Quality & Validation

  • Filtered invalid coordinates and placeholder values
  • Identified unmatched crash points from spatial join
  • Analyzed boundary issues and geocoding precision
  • duplicate GEOID rows
  • invalid geometries
  • Final Aggregated Panel: GEOID × year × month

Step 2: Exploratory Analysis

  • crash_count distribution
  • crash_density by tract
  • crash patterns by month/year
  • roadway features vs crash count
  • traffic signal density vs crash count
  • demographics vs crash density

Step 3: Modeling

  • Regression: Poisson, Negative Binomial (for count)
  • Tree-based: XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest
  • Classification: Logistic Regression (baseline), high-risk prediction
  • Evaluation: PR-AUC (primary), ROC-AUC

11. Recommended Modeling Direction

This project is not focused on predicting future crashes or claims. Instead, the goal is to examine whether auto claim or crash patterns are spatially associated with roadway network characteristics and demographic composition.

The key research question is:

Are higher crash or auto-claim concentrations associated with roadway network conditions, and do these patterns disproportionately affect census tracts with higher Black and Hispanic populations?

The analysis will focus on:

  1. Measuring crash / claim density by census tract
  2. Comparing roadway network features across tracts
  3. Examining whether tracts with higher Black and Hispanic population shares also show higher crash / claim concentration
  4. Testing whether roadway network conditions help explain these disparities

Recommended analytical methods:

  • Correlation analysis
  • Group comparison by demographic share
  • Spatial visualization / choropleth maps
  • Regression analysis for association, not prediction
  • Crash density or claim density as outcome
  • Roadway features and demographic shares as explanatory variables

Dependent variable: crash_count, crash_density, auto_claim_density

Main explanatory variables: Black population share, Hispanic population share, roadway network density, intersection density, traffic signal density, speed limit, AADT, bike lane / sidewalk / bus lane indicators, road condition measures

Note: The purpose is not to predict individual crashes or claims. The purpose is to detect whether roadway infrastructure and demographic patterns are associated with unequal crash or claim burden across DC census tracts.

Modeling:

Negative Binomial Regression with population or area offset

  • crash_count ~ black_share + hispanic_share + roadway_features + traffic_features + controls
  • offset = log(population) or log(area_km2)
  • Interaction terms:
    • black_share × roadway_density
    • hispanic_share × roadway_density
    • black_share × intersection_density
    • hispanic_share × intersection_density

OLS / Linear Regression on Crash Density

  • Target var:
    • crash_density = crash_count / area_km2
    • claim_density = claim_count / area_km2
  • crash_density ~ black_share + hispanic_share + roadway_density + intersection_density + signal_density + AADT + speed_limit

Spatial Lag / Spatial Error Model

  • Crash risk could be closed in two or more adjacent tracts
  • Advanced models: …

12. Validation Strategy

  • TimeSeriesSplit to preserve temporal order
  • Avoid data leakage
  • Evaluate across folds

12. Next Steps

  • Finalize boundary threshold handling (e.g., 250m rule)
  • Refine feature set and remove noise
  • Train and compare models
  • Interpret feature importance (SHAP)
  • Develop policy insights

13. Summary

  • acs_demo is the spatial backbone
  • All datasets aggregated to GEOID level
  • Avoids duplication and ensures modeling consistency
  • Supports scalable spatial-temporal analysis

This project uses acs_demo as the spatial backbone because it contains both census tract boundaries and ACS demographic attributes. Crashes, traffic signals, and roadway blocks are spatially joined to acs_demo, aggregated to GEOID, and then merged into one tract-level modeling dataset. This avoids direct many-to-many spatial joins that would otherwise duplicate crash records and distort model results.
